title: Dynamical correlations in a dissipative XXZ spin chain
authors: Cătălin Paşcu Moca, Doru Sticlet, Ovidiu I. Pâţu, Balazs Dóra
abstract: We study dynamical spin correlations in a dissipative XXZ spin chain subject to uniform local spin-loss and pumping. Starting from a mixed steady state that is featureless albeit possessing finite magnetization, rich dynamics emerges in time-dependent two-point correlators evaluated on top of it. For unitary evolution in which the reservoir is absent, the longitudinal correlators reproduce the established hierarchy of spin-transport universality classes - ballistic, Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) superdiffusive, and diffusive - across the phase diagram. However, for finite magnetization, additional ballistic light cone propagation gets superimposed on the previous universality classes, arising from magnon propagation.The transverse correlator displays very fast, exponential decay of correlations without wavefront propagation in the easy-plane case. At the isotropic point, it follows KPZ scaling due to $SU(2)$ symmetry, while in the easy-axis regime, it is characterized by ballistic spreading of correlations. Under full Lindbladian dynamics, the universality classes are preserved at early times, while the correlations acquire an overall exponential damping in the long-time limit. In terms of methods, we have used vectorized TEBD for numerical simulations and exact analytical results obtained via a Pfaffian representation and the third-quantization framework for the noninteracting XX case.
summary: This paper explores how dissipation modifies the spreading of correlations in an XXZ spin chain. It demonstrates that while dissipation eventually leads to exponential decay, the underlying transport universality classes and light-cone structures remain intact during the early-time dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates how dissipation (spin loss and pumping) affects the dynamical spin correlations and transport universality classes in a 1D XXZ spin chain.
main_result: The fundamental transport universality classes (ballistic, KPZ, and diffusive) and the light-cone structure are preserved at short times, while dissipation causes exponential damping of correlations at long times.
method: The authors use vectorized Time-Evolving Block Decimation (TEBD) for interacting regimes and a third-quantization framework with a Pfaffian representation for the noninteracting limit.
model_or_system: A 1D spin-1/2 XXZ spin chain subject to Lindblad dynamics with uniform local spin-loss and pumping, leading to a featureless product state steady state.
key_observables: Longitudinal and transverse two-point spin correlators, and longitudinal spin autocorrelation.
important_parameters: Anisotropy (Delta), spin fugacity (zeta), dissipation rate (Gamma), and the crossover time scale (t*).
main_assumptions: The steady state is assumed to be a Gaussian product state; numerical results are subject to bond dimension and time-step constraints.
figures_summary: Figures show magnetization vs. fugacity, spatiotemporal structures of longitudinal correlations (identifying light cones and dynamical exponents), and the decay of transverse/longitudinal autocorrelations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dissipative XXZ model, establishes the steady state, analyzes unitary evolution (quench) dynamics, extends the study to full Lindbladian dynamics, and provides exact analytical benchmarks for the noninteracting limit.
why_interesting: It provides critical insights into the robustness of many-body transport phenomena (like KPZ scaling) and the stability of causal light-cone structures in the presence of environmental decoherence, which is central to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Average local magnetization ⟨σz l ⟩SS versus fugacity ζ for several anisotropy parameters ∆, obtained with the vectorized TEBD method. All symbols collapse onto the analytical prediction Eq. (9) (solid line), confirming that the SS structure is governed entirely by the gain–loss balance ζ = γp/γl and is independent of J and ∆. System size is L = 20 and bond dimension χ = 128. The results have been checked for convergence in both parameters.

Figure 1 benchmarks the SS against vectorized TEBD simulations for different values of ∆. The numerical data collapse exactly onto Eq. (9) for all anisotropies, confirming that coherent exchange interactions play no role in determining the steady-state magnetization and that the SS structure is set entirely by the dissipative rates through ζ and independent of ∆. At the special point ζ = 1 (γl = γp), the SS reduces to the infinite-temperature state, ρSS ∝1, with vanishing average magnetization ⟨σz l ⟩SS = 0. Although static correlations are completely suppressed in the SS for any ζ, nontrivial physics emerges in the time-dependent correlation functions of operators evaluated on top of this...

Figure 2: (a) Density plot showing the time evolution of the connected longitudinal correlation function ⟨σz x(t) σz 0⟩(H) c after the quench. The data is displayed on a logarithmic scale to enhance the visibility of the ballistic light-cone structure. (b) Temporal evolution of the autocorrelation at the central site, comparing TEBD results with the analytical expression Eq. (16). Simulation parameters: L = 129, ζ = 2.

Figure 3: Time evolution of the longitudinal autocorrelation ⟨σz x(t) σz 0⟩(H) c for several values of ∆and fugacity ζ (indicated in each panel). The data is displayed on a logarithmic color scale to enhance the visibility of the light-cone structure. Dashed lines mark the light-cone boundaries. The dynamical exponent z governing the cone is ballistic (z = 1) for ∆< 1, superdiffusive (z = 3/2) at ∆= 1, and diffusive (z = 2) for ∆> 1. For ∆= 2.0, ζ = 2.0 two distinct fronts are visible, signaling coexistence of ballistic and diffusive modes. Data shown on a logarithmic color scale.

Figure 4: Long-time decay of the central longitudinal autocorrelation for different fugacities ζ. The asymptotic behavior is consistent with ⟨σz 0(t) σz 0⟩(H) c ∼(Jt)−1/z irrespective of ζ, confirming that the dynamical exponent z is independent of the initial magnetization and is solely determined by the anisotropy ∆.
